policy_statement: Reposition/rehabilitate riparian galleries (planting/sowing indigenous species) and consolidate/recover slopes and riverbanks using natural engineering techniques for erosion control.
mechanism_description: It rehabilitates riparian vegetation with indigenous species and uses natural engineering to stabilize slopes and riverbanks, reducing erosion and strengthening river corridor resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (riparian vegetation + bioengineering) → ↓ erosion/landslide susceptibility + ↑ riverbank stability → ↓ climate-hazard impacts (flooding, sedimentation, infrastructure damage)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Land and river-corridor management shifts to restoring native riparian vegetation and using nature-based/bioengineering stabilization (e.g., live staking, fascines, vegetated revetments) instead of—or alongside—hard engineering.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Root reinforcement and vegetative cover increase soil cohesion and infiltration, slow overland flow, dissipate stream energy at banks, trap sediments, and improve channel/floodplain roughness and ecological integrity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced erosion, bank failure, and sediment loads, leading to lower damages and service disruptions during climate-amplified floods/storms (and improved resilience of riverine ecosystems under hydrologic extremes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and vulnerability to flooding/erosion impacts through stabilized riparian zones and more resilient river corridors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem restoration/bioengineering improves riparian function and physical stability, which reduces erosion and climate-driven hazard impacts—an ecosystem-based resilience pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although restored vegetation can store carbon (mitigation) and improve water-use efficiency (resource efficiency), the mechanism description centers on erosion control and corridor resilience (risk reduction), which is most consistent with Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy using this mechanism were framed/quantified primarily for carbon sequestration (e.g., carbon credits) it could be mitigation at the policy level, but that would be a different dominant pathway than “erosion control and resilience.”  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that restores riparian vegetation and applies bioengineering for stabilization is fundamentally an ecosystem-based intervention aimed at reducing erosion and increasing system resilience to hydrologic extremes.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary tagging (e.g., adaptation co-benefits vs. mitigation co-benefits magnitude) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Possible co-primary mitigation where riparian restoration is explicitly designed/credited for carbon sequestration; possible hard-infrastructure dominance if “bioengineering” is minor and grey stabilization is primary (would shift away from NBS). Otherwise straightforward.
key_indicators: “Reposition/rehabilitation of the riparian gallery”; “planting and/or sowing of indigenous species”; “Consolidation and recovery of slopes and riverbanks”; “natural engineering techniques”
co_benefits: Biodiversity/habitat improvement; water quality improvement via sediment trapping; potential local cooling/shade along waterways; landscape/amenity benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a “planning-strategy” if presented as a plan/policy rather than implementation; however, the wording is action-oriented (rehabilitation/consolidation works), so it is best treated as an investment/project instrument.
